title: Writing Professional Memos and Reports
description: Create professional memos and reports with proper structure, table of contents, page numbering, and section breaks.
objectives: Understand memo format (To, From, Date, Re)
Structure a report with headings and sections
Add page numbers and section breaks
Use professional formatting for internal communications
content:
Memos in the Workplace

A memo (memorandum) is a short internal document used to communicate information within an organization. It is more formal than an email but shorter than a full report. A memo has a standard format:

  • To: The recipient(s)
  • From: The sender
  • Date: The date of writing
  • Re: The subject (short and clear)

The body of the memo is direct and concise. It states the purpose in the first sentence, provides details in the middle, and ends with any action required.

Workplace Memo Example

OFFICE MEMO

Date: 24th March, 2024

From: Public Relations Manager

To: All Senior Staff

Re: SALARIES

I am pleased to inform you that following the sit-down strike and the protracted negotiations put in by your Union representatives, the employment board in its last meeting agreed to increase your pay by 15%.

However, I regret to inform you that the increments which should have been effected immediately will be delayed for one month due to the breakdown in our computer system. We are however working around the clock to ensure that you are paid fully including your arrears next month.

Happy Easter.

Reports

A report is a longer document that presents information, analysis, and recommendations. A professional report includes:

  1. Cover page with title, author, date, and organization
  2. Table of Contents (auto-generated by Word)
  3. Introduction explaining the purpose
  4. Body sections with headings and subheadings
  5. Conclusion and recommendations
  6. Appendices if needed

Table of Contents and Page Numbers

To create a table of contents, first apply heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2) to your section titles. Then go to References > Table of Contents and choose a style. Word automatically updates the page numbers when you add or remove content. For page numbering, go to Insert > Page Number and choose a location. Use section breaks if you want different numbering styles (e.g., Roman numerals for the introduction, Arabic numerals for the main content).

exercise: Create a memo in Word addressed to 'All Staff' from 'HR Manager' dated today. Subject: 'Office Relocation.' Explain that the office will move to a new building on 1st August. Include two paragraphs: one explaining the reason, one listing what staff need to do (pack personal items, update addresses). Format with proper memo headers. Then create a 3-page report titled 'Computer Lab Status Report' with a cover page, table of contents, two headings, and page numbers. Save both documents.

quiz.question: What does 'Re:' mean in a memo?
quiz.explanation: In a memo, 'Re:' stands for 'Regarding' and indicates the subject or topic of the memo.

quiz.question: How do you create a Table of Contents in Word?
options: Apply heading styles and use References > Table of Contents
quiz.explanation: Word automatically generates a Table of Contents based on the heading styles you apply throughout the document.

quiz.question: Why are section breaks useful in a long report?
options: To apply different headers, footers, or page numbering in different parts
quiz.explanation: Section breaks allow different formatting in different parts of a document, such as Roman numerals for the introduction and regular numbers for the main content.

quiz.question: Which of these should be included in a professional report?
options: Cover page, table of contents, headings, conclusion, and page numbers
quiz.explanation: A professional report includes a cover page, table of contents, structured headings, body content, conclusion, recommendations, and page numbers.
